threats.title: cook() Solvency Check Bypass via Unhandled Action Codes
threats.hypothesis_description: The cook() function's solvency check can be entirely bypassed by appending an unhandled action code (e.g., ACTION_ACCRUE=8, or any value like 0, 1, 3, 9) after ACTION_BORROW or ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL. The unhandled action falls to the else branch calling _additionalCookAction(), which returns a default-initialized CookStatus{needsSolvencyCheck:false, hasAccrued:false}. The line `status = returnStatus` overwrites the previously set needsSolvencyCheck=true, causing the final solvency check to be skipped entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect in the most critical financial control of the lending protocol. The solvency check is the sole mechanism preventing undercollateralized borrowing. Its bypass enables unlimited borrowing against minimal or no collateral, draining the entire MIM supply from the Cauldron.

proof_of_signal.pattern: else branch calls _additionalCookAction which returns default CookStatus; status = returnStatus overwrites needsSolvencyCheck=true set by ACTION_BORROW

preconditions: Any CauldronV4 or BlastCauldronV4 clone is deployed and funded with MIM
Attacker has some collateral in BentoBox or uses skim mode
No override of _additionalCookAction that preserves CookStatus

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: status = returnStatus where returnStatus is default CookStatus{false,false} from empty _additionalCookAction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: cook([ACTION_BORROW, 0], ...) - second action is unhandled, resets needsSolvencyCheck to false
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker borrows entire MIM balance of Cauldron with minimal collateral

economic_questions: What is the total MIM supply available across all deployed Cauldron clones?
Does BlastCauldronV4 or any other subclass override _additionalCookAction to fix this?
Can the attack be executed atomically in a single transaction?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: cook([5,0], [0,0], [borrow_abi_data, 0x]) and verify no solvency check
Verify _additionalCookAction returns default CookStatus in CauldronV4 base
Confirm ACTION_ACCRUE(8) has no handler in the if-else chain

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal collateral (1 wei of collateral share sufficient)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire MIM balance of targeted Cauldron clone

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time while Cauldron has MIM
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete drain of Cauldron MIM supply

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All Cauldron MIM depositors and the protocol treasury
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of MIM lent to Cauldron
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All MIM in all CauldronV4 clones

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: ACTION_ACCRUE(8) defined but unhandled; actions 0,1,3,9 also unhandled
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace CookStatus through cook() with [ACTION_BORROW, 0] sequence

threats.title: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves() Always Returns (0,0) Due to Missing Return Statement
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLpAggregator._getReserves() declares local variables via tuple destructuring that shadow the unnamed return variables. The function never assigns to or returns the actual return variables, so it always returns (0,0). This causes latestAnswer() to always return 0, making any LP token valued at zero when used as a Cauldron oracle.
threats.investigation_rationale: If this aggregator is deployed as a Cauldron oracle for LP collateral, exchangeRate=0 means collateral is treated as infinitely valuable. All solvency checks pass trivially, enabling unlimited undercollateralized borrowing. Alternatively, if the oracle integration inverts the price, all positions appear insolvent, triggering mass unfair liquidations.
deployment_assumptions: MagicLpAggregator is used as oracle for a Cauldron with LP collateral
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script shows MagicLpAggregator deployment, but documentation states 'MagicLpAggregator: An oracle used to price MagicLP collateral for Cauldrons'

proof_of_signal.pattern: Local variables (baseReserve, quoteReserve) shadow unnamed return values; no return statement; function returns (0,0)

preconditions: MagicLpAggregator deployed without overriding _getReserves
Used as price feed for Cauldron LP collateral valuation
MagicLP pair has non-zero reserves and totalSupply

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No manipulation needed - the oracle is permanently broken by code defect
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If exchangeRate=0 in Cauldron: unlimited borrowing or mass unfair liquidations

economic_questions: Is MagicLpAggregator actually deployed and wired to any Cauldron?
Does any subclass override _getReserves to fix the bug?
How is the oracle integrated: does exchangeRate=0 enable overborrowing or force liquidations?
validation_requirements: Call _getReserves() and verify it returns (0,0)
Call latestAnswer() on a pool with reserves and verify it returns 0
Trace exchangeRate=0 through CauldronV4._isSolvent to determine impact direction

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire MIM supply of affected Cauldrons

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately upon deployment of affected Cauldron
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of Cauldron MIM or total loss of LP collateral

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LP collateral depositors and MIM lenders in affected Cauldron

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify _getReserves return behavior with Solidity compiler
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test latestAnswer() output with live pair data

threats.title: FeeCollectable.setFeeParameters Validates Old State Instead of New Input
threats.hypothesis_description: setFeeParameters() checks `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of `if (_feeBips > BIPS)`, validating the OLD state variable rather than the NEW input parameter. This allows setting feeBips to any value (including >100%) as long as the current feeBips is valid. Once set above BIPS, subsequent calls to setFeeParameters revert (old value now fails check), permanently locking the invalid fee. calculateFees() will then underflow or extract more than 100% of amounts.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect in fee validation that any authorized fee operator would trigger during normal operations. The wrong variable name means the validation is completely non-functional for new values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Checks 'feeBips > BIPS' (old state) instead of '_feeBips > BIPS' (new input parameter)

preconditions: Contract inheriting FeeCollectable is deployed
Fee operator calls setFeeParameters with _feeBips > BIPS
Current feeBips <= BIPS (always true initially since feeBips starts at 0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: feeBips set to 20000 (200%): feeAmount = amountIn * 20000 / 10000 = 2*amountIn → underflow in userAmount = amountIn - feeAmount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First call to setFeeParameters always passes validation since old feeBips=0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If feeBips > BIPS: calculateFees underflows or extracts >100%, permanent DOS on subsequent setFeeParameters calls

economic_questions: Which contracts inherit FeeCollectable and are deployed?
What fee values are set via deployment scripts?
What is the downstream impact of calculateFees with feeBips > BIPS?
validation_requirements: Call setFeeParameters(addr, 20000) when feeBips=0, verify it succeeds
Call setFeeParameters again, verify it reverts (permanent lock)
Call calculateFees with feeBips=20000, verify underflow or overflow

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on fee extraction path; potential DOS of all fee operations
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - primarily DOS and correctness issue

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent lock of fee configuration; potential fund extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users of contracts inheriting FeeCollectable
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% if fees exceed amounts; permanent DOS of fee changes
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All funds flowing through fee calculation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: feeBips vs _feeBips variable names in setFeeParameters
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace calculateFees with feeBips > BIPS values

threats.title: Liquidation Double-Computation of allBorrowShare Enables Liquidator Underpayment
threats.hypothesis_description: In CauldronV4.liquidate(), allBorrowShare is computed twice via bentoBox.toShare(). Between computations, collateral is transferred and the swapper callback executes. A malicious swapper can manipulate BentoBox MIM totals (e.g., deposit MIM to inflate elastic), causing the second toShare to return fewer shares for the same amount. The liquidator pays the second (lower) share amount while the first (higher) amount was used to determine swapper obligations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The liquidator freely chooses the swapper. A purpose-built swapper can manipulate BentoBox state during the callback to reduce the liquidator's payment obligation, creating bad debt in the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: allBorrowShare computed at line ~400, swapper.swap called at ~406, allBorrowShare recomputed at ~409 with potentially different BentoBox totals

preconditions: Insolvent positions exist in the Cauldron
Attacker deploys a custom swapper contract
Swapper can interact with BentoBox during callback to modify MIM totals

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: First: allBorrowShare = toShare(MIM, allBorrowAmount, true) = X; After swap manipulates totals: allBorrowShare = toShare(MIM, allBorrowAmount, true) = Y where Y < X
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Swapper deposits MIM into BentoBox during callback → elastic increases → fewer shares needed per amount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Liquidator pays Y < X shares, receiving full collateral but underpaying MIM

economic_questions: How much can BentoBox MIM totals be shifted during a single swap callback?
What is the maximum bad debt that can be created per liquidation?
Does the first allBorrowShare serve any purpose, or is it dead code?
validation_requirements: Build malicious swapper that deposits MIM during callback
Measure allBorrowShare difference before and after swap
Calculate net profit to liquidator vs bad debt created

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Flash loan for MIM deposit manipulation (requires validation)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Difference between first and second allBorrowShare conversions
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on BentoBox total manipulation magnitude

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Creates bad debt in Cauldron proportional to manipulation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: BentoBox MIM totals elastic/base ratio sensitivity
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare toShare results with modified vs unmodified totals

threats.title: Router._validateDecimals Underflow Prevents Valid Pool Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: Router._validateDecimals() performs `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` as a uint8 subtraction. When baseDecimals > quoteDecimals, this underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8+. This prevents creating pools through the Router where the base token has more decimals than the quote token, even if the difference is within the acceptable 12-decimal range.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect that blocks legitimate pool creation for certain token pairs. The check should use absolute difference. Any pair where base has more decimals than quote (e.g., 18-decimal base with 8-decimal quote) cannot be created via the Router.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint8 subtraction quoteDecimals - baseDecimals underflows when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

preconditions: User attempts to create a pool where baseToken.decimals() > quoteToken.decimals()
Using Router.createPool or Router.createPoolETH

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint8(6) - uint8(18) underflows to 244 in unchecked, reverts in Solidity 0.8+
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack vector but a functionality defect blocking pool creation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; DOS on pool creation for valid token pairs

economic_questions: Which planned token pairs have baseDecimals > quoteDecimals?
Can users bypass Router and create pools directly via Factory?
Does this affect MIM/USDB pair specifically?
validation_requirements: Call createPool with base=18 decimals, quote=8 decimals tokens
Verify revert due to uint8 underflow
Check if Factory.create allows the same pair without Router

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users wanting to create pools with baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check MIM(18)/USDB(18) and other planned pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test uint8 subtraction behavior with Solidity 0.8.20

threats.title: BlastOnboardingBoot._claimable Treats MIM and USDB as Equal Value
threats.hypothesis_description: _claimable() computes user pool shares by summing raw MIM and USDB locked amounts: totalLocked = totals[MIM].locked + totals[USDB].locked. Since MIM and USDB have different values (I=0.998, meaning 1 MIM ≈ 0.998 USDB), depositing the less valuable token gives disproportionate shares. If MIM depegs further, an attacker buying cheap MIM and depositing it gets the same claim weight as expensive USDB deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bootstrap share allocation does not account for the value difference between MIM and USDB. This creates an economic incentive to deposit whichever token is cheaper per unit, diluting the more expensive token's depositors.
threats.config_evidence: BlastOnboardingBoot.sol defines I = 0.998 ether, confirming tokens are NOT 1:1

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalLocked = totals[MIM].locked + totals[USDB].locked sums raw amounts of different-valued tokens

preconditions: MIM price differs from USDB price (always true per I=0.998)
Bootstrap has occurred and ready is set
Attacker deposited locked tokens during Opened state

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: userShares = (userMIM + userUSDB) * totalPoolShares / (totalMIM + totalUSDB) treats 1 MIM = 1 USDB
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Buy cheap MIM, deposit locked, claim disproportionate pool shares at USDB depositors' expense
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: At I=0.998: ~0.2% advantage per unit deposited as MIM vs USDB; larger with depeg

economic_questions: What is the actual MIM/USDB price spread during onboarding?
How much total value was deposited in MIM vs USDB?
What is the maximum extraction if MIM depegs to 0.9 USDB?
validation_requirements: Calculate _claimable for user depositing 1000 MIM vs 1000 USDB
Compare actual value received vs fair value share
Model extraction at various MIM depeg levels

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to desired extraction; large deposits amplify advantage
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 0.2% to 10%+ of deposited value depending on MIM depeg
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 0.2% at current I; higher during MIM depeg events
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (deposit during Opened, claim after bootstrap)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During MIM price weakness relative to USDB
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes USDB depositors proportional to MIM price discount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare value-weighted vs amount-weighted share allocation

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards processExpiredLocks Corrupts lastLockIndex via Swap-and-Pop
threats.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks uses swap-and-pop to remove expired locks. When the last element is swapped into a processed position, lastLockIndex is updated to point to the swapped position. This means lastLockIndex now points to an OLDER lock, not the chronologically latest one. Subsequent _createLock calls check if the lock at lastLockIndex has a different unlockTime than nextUnlockTime, and if so, create a NEW lock entry instead of appending to the current epoch's lock. This causes premature maxLocks exhaustion.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users who actively stake with locking each epoch will accumulate more lock entries than expected, eventually hitting maxLocks and being unable to lock more tokens. Their boosted rewards are effectively capped.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Swap-and-pop updates lastLockIndex to point to swapped (older) lock; _createLock at lines 481-498 checks lastLockIndex unlockTime to decide new entry vs append

preconditions: User has multiple active locks at different unlock times
Operator processes a non-last lock, causing swap-and-pop
User subsequently creates new locks in the same or next epoch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lastLockIndex points to lock with old unlockTime after swap; _createLock sees old unlockTime < nextUnlockTime → creates new entry instead of appending
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal operation by operators triggers the corruption; users cannot control lock processing order
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit extraction; users lose ability to lock and earn boosted rewards when maxLocks reached

economic_questions: How many users actively lock every epoch?
What is maxLocks value and how quickly can it be exhausted?
What is the reward loss from being unable to lock?
validation_requirements: Simulate: create 3 locks, process middle one, verify lastLockIndex points to wrong lock
Create new lock after processing, verify extra entry created
Measure epochs until maxLocks hit vs expected

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - functionality defect not direct exploitation
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - reward loss to affected users

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Triggered during normal lock processing operations
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degraded staking UX; potential locked funds inaccessible in expected timeframe

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Active lockers who stake every epoch
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lost boost multiplier on future locks; up to 3x reward reduction
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative lost boosted rewards for affected users

threats.title: MagicLP Uses tx.origin for Fee Rate Determination Instead of msg.sender
threats.hypothesis_description: sellBase(), sellQuote(), and flashLoan() pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate calculation via the FeeRateModel. If the FeeRateModel provides differential rates based on address (e.g., whitelisted traders get lower fees), attackers can route trades through a whitelisted EOA's transaction context. This also breaks smart contract wallet and account abstraction compatibility.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin for fee determination allows fee tier spoofing. If any address gets preferential rates, attackers can exploit this by having that EOA initiate transactions containing their trades.
deployment_assumptions: FeeRateModel provides differential rates based on trader address
threats.config_evidence: MIMSwap.s.sol deploys FeeRateModel with implementation FeeRateModelImpl; actual rate logic depends on implementation

proof_of_signal.pattern: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender

preconditions: FeeRateModel returns different rates for different addresses
A whitelisted or preferential-rate address exists
Attacker can route transactions through that address's context

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getFeeRate(tx.origin, _LP_FEE_RATE_) returns preferential rate for whitelisted EOA
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Route swap through whitelisted EOA to get lower fees

economic_questions: Does FeeRateModelImpl differentiate rates by address?
What is the maximum fee differential exploitable?
How much volume passes through MagicLP swaps?
validation_requirements: Review FeeRateModelImpl.getFeeRate logic for address-dependent rates
Test sellBase with different tx.origin addresses
Compare fee outputs for different addresses

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Trade capital only; no extra cost
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on rate model; potentially 0% if flat rate

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: LP providers (reduced fee revenue), protocol treasury
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced LP returns proportional to fee leakage
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative fee differential across all exploited trades

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare fees with msg.sender vs tx.origin for various addresses

threats.title: CauldronV4 Blacklist Becomes Stale After BentoBox Ownership Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: CauldronV4's constructor and init() blacklist the BentoBox owner at deployment time via `blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] = true`. If BentoBox ownership is later transferred, the new owner is NOT blacklisted. An attacker using cook() ACTION_CALL can target the new BentoBox owner address, potentially executing unauthorized operations if the new owner contract has callable functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The blacklist is a security control preventing cook() from calling sensitive addresses. If it becomes stale, the trust boundary is violated. The BentoBox owner has elevated privileges over user funds.
deployment_assumptions: BentoBox ownership may be transferred after Cauldron deployment
threats.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol shows BoringOwnable(address(blastBox)).transferOwnership(owner, true, false) - ownership IS transferred post-deploy

proof_of_signal.pattern: blacklistedCallees[Owned(address(bentoBox)).owner()] set only at init time; not updated on ownership transfer

preconditions: BentoBox ownership transferred after Cauldron init
New BentoBox owner address has callable functions useful for exploitation
Attacker uses cook() ACTION_CALL targeting new owner address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: blacklistedCallees captures BentoBox owner at init time only; stale after transfer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: ACTION_CALL to new BentoBox owner (multisig/governor) with crafted calldata
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depends on new owner contract capabilities; could enable unauthorized BentoBox operations

economic_questions: What is the new BentoBox owner address after transfer?
What functions does the new owner contract expose?
Can those functions affect BentoBox balances or approvals?
validation_requirements: Verify deployment scripts transfer BentoBox ownership
Check if new owner address is blacklisted in any Cauldron clone
Test ACTION_CALL to new owner address

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on exploitable functions on new owner
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Unknown until new owner contract analyzed

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Depends on new owner contract capabilities

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All Cauldron users if BentoBox operations compromised
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All BentoBox TVL if new owner can be called maliciously

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check blacklistedCallees for new owner address

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards.recover() Can Drain Undistributed Reward Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The recover() function only restricts skimming for the stakingToken. For any other token (including reward tokens), the owner can withdraw the ENTIRE balance with no checks against pending/undistributed rewards. This allows the owner to drain reward tokens that have been notified via notifyRewardAmount but not yet claimed by users.
threats.investigation_rationale: Reward tokens are held by the contract and distributed over time via rewardRate. The owner can call recover(rewardToken, fullBalance) to withdraw all reward tokens, leaving the rewardRate pointing to non-existent tokens. Users' earned() will show rewards they can never claim.
threats.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows staking.addReward(arb) - reward tokens are configured

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only checks skim limit for stakingToken; no protection for reward token balances against withdrawal

preconditions: Owner key compromised or malicious owner
Reward tokens have been notified and partially distributed
Users have unclaimed rewards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: recover(rewardToken, type(uint256).max) withdraws all reward tokens with no pending reward check
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner calls recover for each reward token
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All undistributed + unclaimed reward tokens extracted

economic_questions: What is the total value of reward tokens held by the contract?
Is the owner a multisig with adequate security?
Can this be mitigated by checking reward token balance against pending rewards?
validation_requirements: Call recover(rewardToken, balance) as owner and verify success
Verify users can no longer claim rewards after drain
Check if staking principal is affected

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After large reward notification, before user claims
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of all pending rewards for all stakers

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of all accrued but unclaimed rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total reward token balance in contract

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current reward token balances vs pending distributions
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no implicit protection exists in recover for reward tokens

threats.title: BlastOnboardingBoot ready Flag Can Be Toggled to Disrupt Claiming and Swap Staking Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: setReady() can toggle the ready flag between true and false. setStaking() is only blocked when ready=true. A malicious or compromised owner can: (1) setReady(true) → users claim to staking contract A, (2) setReady(false) → setStaking(B) to new contract, (3) setReady(true) → remaining users claim to staking contract B. Early claimers are in contract A with no rewards; late claimers in contract B.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ready flag should be monotonically true once set. Its reversibility allows splitting users across different staking contracts, potentially stranding early claimers' funds with no reward streams.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setReady(bool _ready) allows toggling; setStaking blocked only when ready=true; no one-way enforcement

preconditions: Owner key compromised or malicious owner action
Some users have already claimed to original staking contract
Owner toggles ready and swaps staking contract

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early claimers lose access to reward streams; their LP is stranded

economic_questions: What is the total LP value at stake during claiming?
Can early claimers unstake from contract A and restake to B?
Is the staking contract's withdrawal permissionless?
validation_requirements: Test setReady(true)→claim→setReady(false)→setStaking(newAddr)→setReady(true)
Verify early claimers are in different staking contract than late claimers
Check if early claimers can withdraw and move to new staking

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Early claimers' LP share value if rewards redirected
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Full reward stream value for stranded users

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Split user base across staking contracts

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Early claimers staked to original contract
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lost reward streams on staked LP
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total LP shares claimed before staking swap

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Can LockingMultiRewards withdrawals bypass operator locks?
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify state transitions allow ready toggling
